title: Vector Arithmetic
blurb: Adding tip-to-tail, scaling, subtracting, vectors inside triangles and parallelograms, and the section formula.

lessons.title: Adding Vectors
lessons.concreteComparison: Walk 3 km east, then 4 km north. Where are you? One 5-km displacement from where you started — the two legs ADD into a single trip. Vector addition is trip-chaining: tip-to-tail in the picture, component-by-component in the arithmetic.
lessons.objective: Add vectors componentwise, chain displacements tip-to-tail, and read the triangle and parallelogram pictures of a sum.
concept: Componentwise, addition couldn't be flatter: $(x_1, y_1) + (x_2, y_2) = (x_1 + x_2,\; y_1 + y_2)$. Easts add with easts, norths with norths.
Geometrically it's **tip-to-tail**: draw $\vec{u}$, start $\vec{v}$ where $\vec{u}$ ends; the sum runs from the first tail to the last tip. Chain as many as you like — the sum closes the whole trip.

Slide $\vec{v}$'s tail back to $\vec{u}$'s tail instead and the two arrows span a **parallelogram**; the sum is its diagonal. Triangle picture and parallelogram picture are the same addition — physics prefers the parallelogram (forces pull from one point), navigation the triangle (legs happen in sequence).

The path-closing identity $\overrightarrow{AB} + \overrightarrow{BC} = \overrightarrow{AC}$ is vector addition's most-used consequence: middle letters cancel. It turns geometry proofs into letter bookkeeping.
lessons.keyIdea: Add components; picture it tip-to-tail (triangle) or tail-to-tail (parallelogram); AB + BC = AC — middle letters cancel.

facts.title: Componentwise
facts.explanation: Easts with easts, norths with norths.

workedExamples.statement: Compute $(2, 3) + (4, -1)$ and the magnitude of the sum.
A hiker walks $3$ km east, then $4$ km north. Find the resultant displacement and its magnitude.
workedExamples.solution: $(3, 0) + (0, 4) = (3, 4)$ — a single $5$-km displacement. The two legs and the resultant form the $3$–$4$–$5$ triangle.

workedExamples.statement: Forces $(5, 0)$ and $(-2, 6)$ act on one object. Find the net force and its magnitude.
workedExamples.solution: Net $= (3, 6)$; $|(3,6)| = \sqrt{45} = 3\sqrt{5}$. In the parallelogram picture, the net force is the diagonal from the shared tail.

commonMistakes.correction: |u + v| is NOT |u| + |v| unless the vectors point the same way. 3 east + 4 north is 5, not 7 — direction always has a say.
The second vector starts where the first ENDS. Starting both at the origin draws the parallelogram picture — fine, but then the sum is the diagonal, not the second arrow.

steps.title: Trips chain
steps.body: Walk one displacement, then another — the net effect is a single displacement. Vector addition just formalizes trip-chaining, and the arithmetic is as flat as it gets: add the easts, add the norths.

steps.title: Tip-to-tail, live
steps.teach: The second arrow starts where the first ends, and the resultant springs from first tail to last tip. Slide them tail-to-tail instead and the same sum appears as the parallelogram's diagonal — two pictures, one addition.

steps.title: Middle letters cancel
steps.explanation: Go A→B, then B→C: net trip A→C. The shared middle letter B cancels — the workhorse identity of vector geometry.

steps.title: Estimate the diagonal first
steps.body: Before computing, eyeball the parallelogram: the sum must land BETWEEN the two directions, closer to the longer arrow. If your arithmetic says otherwise, a sign slipped.

steps.title: The oldest vector law
steps.body: The parallelogram of forces was stated by Simon Stevin in the 1580s — three centuries before anyone wrote the word 'vector'. Sailors and engineers were adding arrows long before mathematicians named them.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Add componentwise; never add bare magnitudes.
Tip-to-tail (sequence) and parallelogram (shared tail) draw the same sum.

lessons.title: Scalar Multiples & Subtraction
lessons.concreteComparison: Double the recipe: every ingredient doubles. Scaling a vector scales BOTH components — the arrow stretches, direction untouched (or U-turned, if the number is negative). And subtraction is nothing new: $\vec{u} - \vec{v}$ is just $\vec{u} + (-\vec{v})$, adding the U-turn.
lessons.objective: Scale vectors, combine scaling with addition, subtract vectors, and solve simple vector equations.
concept: Scalar multiplication stretches: $k(x, y) = (kx, ky)$. The length scales by $|k|$; a negative $k$ additionally reverses the arrow. This is exactly the parallel test from Unit 1 read forwards.

Subtraction is addition in disguise: $\vec{u} - \vec{v} = \vec{u} + (-\vec{v})$, i.e. subtract componentwise. Geometrically $\vec{u} - \vec{v}$ runs **from $\vec{v}$'s tip to $\vec{u}$'s tip** when both start at one point — remember it as "target minus start".

Linear combinations like $3\vec{u} - 2\vec{v}$ are the everyday currency: scale first, then add. And vector equations solve like ordinary algebra — $2\vec{x} + (3, -1) = (7, 5)$ peels apart one operation at a time, because every rule you'd hope for (commutativity, distributivity) actually holds.
lessons.keyIdea: k(x, y) = (kx, ky); u − v = u + (−v), drawn tip-of-v to tip-of-u; vector equations solve like ordinary algebra.

facts.title: Subtraction
facts.explanation: Componentwise; geometrically, from v's tip to u's tip.

facts.title: Algebra holds
facts.explanation: Distribute, collect, solve — like numbers, coordinate by coordinate.

workedExamples.statement: With $\vec{u} = (5, 2)$ and $\vec{v} = (1, 3)$ drawn from one point, find the vector from $\vec{v}$'s tip to $\vec{u}$'s tip.

workedExamples.solution: $2\vec{x} = (4, 6)$, so $\vec{x} = (2, 3)$ — vector algebra peels exactly like number algebra.

commonMistakes.correction: 3·(2, −1) = (6, −3), both lanes. Scaling one component doesn't stretch the arrow — it bends it into a different direction.

commonMistakes.text: Drawing u − v from u's tip to v's tip.
commonMistakes.correction: u − v points AT u: from v's tip to u's tip. 'Target minus start' — the answer aims at the first-named vector.

steps.title: Stretch, flip, combine
steps.body: Multiply a vector by $k$ and both components scale: the arrow stretches by $|k|$, and flips if $k < 0$. Combine with addition and you get **linear combinations** like $3\vec{u} - 2\vec{v}$ — the everyday currency of vector work.

steps.title: Target minus start
steps.body: $\vec{u} - \vec{v}$ is just $\vec{u} + (-\vec{v})$ — subtract componentwise. The picture: draw both from one point, and $\vec{u} - \vec{v}$ runs from $\vec{v}$'s tip to $\vec{u}$'s tip, pointing AT the vector named first. It's the same tip-minus-tail from Unit 1: $\overrightarrow{AB} = B - A$.
beats: u − v: componentwise, arrow points at u
vector equations solve like number algebra

steps.title: Scale it
steps.explanation: Both components take the $-3$: $(-6, 15)$. The arrow is three times longer and reversed.

steps.title: Subtract
steps.explanation: $(4 - (-1),\; -2 - 3) = (5, -5)$. The double negative in the first lane is where fingers slip.

steps.title: Scale before you add
steps.body: In $3\vec{u} - 2\vec{v}$, finish both scalings BEFORE combining — writing the two scaled vectors down explicitly costs five seconds and prevents the half-scaled hybrid errors that cost the whole problem.

steps.title: Why 'scalar'?
steps.body: The word comes from the Latin scala — ladder, scale. Scalars SCALE vectors: that's their whole job description, and the name never lets you forget it.

steps.title: What sticks
points: $k(x, y) = (kx, ky)$: stretch by $|k|$, flip if negative.
Vector equations peel like ordinary algebra.

lessons.title: Vectors in Triangles & Parallelograms
lessons.concreteComparison: A parallelogram is a vector machine: pick corner $A$, call the sides $\vec{u} = \overrightarrow{AB}$ and $\vec{v} = \overrightarrow{AD}$, and EVERYTHING in the figure — both diagonals, every midpoint, every median — is some combination of $\vec{u}$ and $\vec{v}$. Exams hand you a figure and ask you to speak its vector language.
lessons.objective: Express diagonals, midpoints, and medians of triangles and parallelograms in terms of two side vectors, and compute their lengths from components.
concept: In parallelogram $ABCD$ with $\vec{u} = \overrightarrow{AB}$ and $\vec{v} = \overrightarrow{AD}$: the diagonal from $A$ is $\overrightarrow{AC} = \vec{u} + \vec{v}$ (walk one side, then the other), and the other diagonal is $\overrightarrow{BD} = \vec{v} - \vec{u}$ (from $B$, undo $\vec{u}$, do $\vec{v}$). Sum and difference — the two diagonals.

Midpoints average: the midpoint $M$ of segment $BC$ satisfies $\overrightarrow{AM} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\overrightarrow{AB} + \overrightarrow{AC})$. That's also the **median** vector of triangle $ABC$ from $A$ — one formula, two names.

The method behind every figure problem: (1) choose a base point, (2) name two independent side vectors, (3) walk to the target along the figure's edges, writing each step as $\pm$ a named vector or a fraction of one. The walk IS the answer.
lessons.keyIdea: Name two side vectors, then walk the figure: diagonals are u + v and v − u; midpoints and medians average: AM = ½(AB + AC).

facts.title: Parallelogram diagonals
facts.explanation: Sum and difference of the two side vectors from A.

facts.title: Median / midpoint
facts.explanation: To the midpoint of BC: average the two side trips.

facts.title: The method
facts.explanation: Every segment in the figure is a ± combination of the named sides.

workedExamples.statement: In parallelogram $ABCD$, $\overrightarrow{AB} = (4, 1)$ and $\overrightarrow{AD} = (1, 3)$. Find the diagonal $\overrightarrow{AC}$ and its length.
Same parallelogram: find the other diagonal $\overrightarrow{BD}$ and its length.
In triangle $ABC$, $\overrightarrow{AB} = (6, 2)$ and $\overrightarrow{AC} = (2, 4)$. Find the median vector $\overrightarrow{AM}$ to side $BC$ and its length.

commonMistakes.text: Writing BD = u − v instead of v − u.
commonMistakes.correction: BD goes from B to D: undo AB (−u), then do AD (+v) — so BD = v − u. Track the walk, not a memorized formula.

commonMistakes.text: Averaging endpoints instead of vectors from the base point.
commonMistakes.correction: The median from A uses vectors FROM A: AM = ½(AB + AC). Mixing in vectors from other corners breaks the bookkeeping.

steps.title: Two vectors run the whole figure
steps.body: Pick a corner of the figure, name the two side vectors leaving it — $\vec{u}$ and $\vec{v}$ — and every other segment becomes a $\pm$ combination of the two. This is the exam's favorite vector genre: speak the figure's language.

steps.title: Diagonals and medians
steps.body: Parallelogram from $A$: diagonal $\overrightarrow{AC} = \vec{u} + \vec{v}$, diagonal $\overrightarrow{BD} = \vec{v} - \vec{u}$ — sum and difference. Triangle median to $BC$'s midpoint: $\overrightarrow{AM} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\overrightarrow{AB} + \overrightarrow{AC})$ — average the trips. Derive each by WALKING: from B, undo $\vec{u}$, do $\vec{v}$: that's $BD$.
beats: AC = u + v — one side then the other
BD = v − u — undo u, do v

steps.title: From B to D
steps.prompt: In parallelogram $ABCD$ with $\vec{u} = \overrightarrow{AB}$, $\vec{v} = \overrightarrow{AD}$, the diagonal $\overrightarrow{BD}$ equals…
steps.explanation: Walk it: B back to A is $-\vec{u}$, then A up to D is $+\vec{v}$. Total: $\vec{v} - \vec{u}$.

steps.title: Average the trips
steps.prompt: Triangle $ABC$ has $\overrightarrow{AB} = (4, 2)$, $\overrightarrow{AC} = (0, 6)$. The median vector $\overrightarrow{AM}$ is…

steps.title: Never memorize — walk
steps.body: Formulas like $BD = \vec{v} - \vec{u}$ are re-derivable in three seconds by walking the figure's edges. Under pressure, the walk is safer than the memory.

steps.title: The centroid, in one line
steps.body: With vectors, the fact that a triangle's three medians meet at one point — two-thirds along each — collapses to a single computation: the centroid is $\tfrac{1}{3}(\vec{a} + \vec{b} + \vec{c})$, perfectly symmetric in the three corners. Classical geometry needs a page; vectors need a line.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Name $\vec{u}, \vec{v}$ at a corner; everything else is a walk.

lessons.title: The Section Formula
lessons.concreteComparison: A midpoint is a 50–50 split. But suppose $P$ sits on $AB$ so that $AP : PB = 2 : 1$ — a 2-to-1 split, two-thirds of the way along. The section formula prices any split: a weighted average of the endpoints, where each endpoint's weight is the ratio part on the FAR side.
lessons.objective: Find the point dividing a segment in a given ratio, recognize the midpoint as the 1:1 case, and recover the ratio from a known dividing point.
concept: If $P$ divides $AB$ with $AP : PB = m : n$, then $$P = \frac{n \cdot A + m \cdot B}{m + n}.$$ Note the cross-over: $B$'s weight is $m$ (the part NEAR $A$'s side of the ratio)… the mnemonic that never fails: the endpoint you're CLOSER to gets the BIGGER weight, and closeness is decided by the ratio.
The midpoint is the formula in its Sunday best: $m : n = 1 : 1$ gives $P = \tfrac{1}{2}(A + B)$ — average the coordinates.

Run it backwards to FIND a ratio: if $P$ is known to sit on $AB$, compare $\overrightarrow{AP}$ with $\overrightarrow{PB}$ componentwise; their scalar ratio is $m : n$. (And if the two vectors aren't parallel, $P$ was never on the line — a built-in lie detector.)
Everything here is the parallel test plus addition — no new machinery, just the most exam-frequent packaging of it.
lessons.keyIdea: AP : PB = m : n ⇒ P = (n·A + m·B)/(m + n); midpoint is the 1:1 case; ratios recover by comparing AP with PB.

facts.title: Section formula
facts.explanation: For AP : PB = m : n — the far part weights each endpoint.

facts.title: Ratio recovery
facts.explanation: Compare the two pieces componentwise to read off m : n.

workedExamples.statement: $P$ divides $AB$ with $AP : PB = 2 : 1$, where $A(1, 2)$ and $B(7, 8)$. Find $P$.
workedExamples.solution: $P = \dfrac{1 \cdot A + 2 \cdot B}{3} = \left(\dfrac{1 + 14}{3}, \dfrac{2 + 16}{3}\right) = (5, 6)$ — two-thirds of the way to $B$, as a 2:1 split demands.

workedExamples.statement: $P(3, 4)$ lies on segment $AB$ with $A(1, 2)$, $B(6, 7)$... wait — check it, then find $AP : PB$.
workedExamples.solution: $\overrightarrow{AP} = (2, 2)$ and $\overrightarrow{PB} = (3, 3)$ — parallel ✓ (so $P$ really is on the line), and $\overrightarrow{AP} = \tfrac{2}{3}\overrightarrow{PB}$. Ratio: $AP : PB = 2 : 3$.

commonMistakes.text: Weighting each endpoint by its own ratio part.
commonMistakes.correction: The weights CROSS: for AP:PB = m:n, A carries n and B carries m. Sanity-check with an extreme: AP:PB = 100:1 must land P almost at B.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to divide by m + n.
commonMistakes.correction: The weights must average, not just add — the denominator m + n is what keeps P between A and B.

tryIt.solution: $P = \dfrac{3A + 1B}{4} = (2, 1)$ — a quarter of the way to $B$.

steps.title: Splits that aren't 50–50
steps.body: Midpoints you know. But geometry constantly needs the point two-thirds along, or splitting $1 : 3$ — centroids, similar-triangle sections, exam questions. One formula prices every split.

steps.title: A weighted average, weights crossed
steps.body: For $AP : PB = m : n$: $$P = \frac{n \cdot A + m \cdot B}{m + n}$$ The weights CROSS — the endpoint you're closer to carries the bigger weight. Check the extremes: $m : n = 1 : 1$ gives the plain average (midpoint); a huge $m$ drags $P$ onto $B$.
beats: weights cross: near endpoint, big weight

steps.title: Which way do weights go?
steps.prompt: $AP : PB = 3 : 1$. Which endpoint gets weight $3$ in the section formula?
options: Neither — 3 goes in the denominator only
steps.explanation: $P = \frac{1 \cdot A + 3 \cdot B}{4}$. A $3:1$ split puts $P$ three-quarters of the way along — close to $B$, so $B$ carries the big weight.

steps.title: Quarter point
steps.explanation: $P = \frac{3A + B}{4} = (3, 2)$ — one quarter of the way, nearest $A$.

steps.title: Test an extreme before trusting
steps.body: Plug $m : n = 1 : 0$ into your formula arrangement — it must return $P = A$... it returns B?! Then your weights are crossed the wrong way. The extreme test settles the crossing in five seconds, every time.

steps.title: Levers knew it first
steps.body: The section formula is Archimedes' law of the lever in coordinates: a mass n at A and a mass m at B balance exactly at P. Physics and geometry agree on where the fulcrum goes.

steps.title: What sticks
points: $AP : PB = m : n \Rightarrow P = \frac{nA + mB}{m + n}$ — weights crossed.
Midpoint = the $1 : 1$ case: average the coordinates.

practice.statement: Triangle $ABC$: $\overrightarrow{AB} = (10, 2)$, $\overrightarrow{AC} = (2, 8)$. Find the median vector $\overrightarrow{AM}$ and its length.
Two forces $(7, -1)$ and $(-3, 5)$ act at a point. Find the magnitude of the resultant.

testYourself.statement: Simplify $\overrightarrow{AB} + \overrightarrow{BC} - \overrightarrow{DC}$ into a single vector between named points.
In triangle $ABC$ with vertices $A(0,0)$, $B(6, 0)$, $C(0, 6)$, find the centroid using $G = \tfrac{1}{3}(A + B + C)$.
